    Test 30: What's the largest prime number you can 3D print without supports?
    Score: 0.00 / 1
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:
2 3D shapes can be said to be stackable if there exists an orientation in which:
- they can be stacked on top of each other without any overhang
- all lowest points on the top model touch the highest points of the lower model.
- the top shapes center of gravity is within the bottom shape.
- the entire stacks center of gravity is within the footprint of the bottom shape.
- they can be 3D printed without supports - ie no cantilevers or bridges.

For example, this sequence is stackable:
- Unit cube.
- Unit cylinder (of d = 1, h = 1)
- 3 legged stool, with a seat diameter of 1.
- 3 fair dice, with a size smaller than any seat leg.

The unit cube can rest in any stable orientation. The cylinder can sit on top with a flat face down.
The stool can be flipped upsidedown and sit on top of the cylinder. The dice can sit on top
of the legs (either one per leg, or stacked on top of each other on a single leg).

Side view of a stack:

      o
      o
      o   Dice

     | |     | |       | |
     | |     | |       | |
     | |     | |       | | Stool. Legs facing up, seat facing down.
     | |     | |       | |
     =====================

     X-------------------X
     |||||||||||||||||||||
     |||||||||||||||||||||
     ||||||||||||||||||||| Cylinder (flat facing up and down)
     |||||||||||||||||||||
     |||||||||||||||||||||
     X-------------------X

     =====================
     =====================
     ===================== Unit cube
     =====================
     =====================
     =====================
     =====================
     =====================
------------------------------- Build plate / ground / Z = 0

So now you understand the concept. Lets try something more advanced!

Using: 
- A 7 segment display font, 
- Fixed diget sizes
- Reading from the ground up,

what is the largest prime number that can be 3D printed in a stack?

2, 3, 5 and 7 can all be printed obviously.

37 can be printed. The 3 can be printed flat first, and then the 7 can be printed rotated
around the y axis so that the short top bar of the 7 is resting on any of the 3 horizontal
segments of the 3.

331 can be printed. One 3 flat. One 3 rotated around y so it has 3 spikes sticking up, and
then a 1 (rotated in y) balenced on the tip of the spikes. 

Can we go any higher?

Clarifications:
- A ban on repeating any sequence of 3 more than once. 
  - So 888 and 6969 substrings are allowed, but 8888 or 69696 are not allowed.
- Only a single diget can be printed at any one z level. So you can't print "138" by printing
  the 1 and 3 concurrently and then use that to support the 8.
- You can rotate in all 3 direction, so you can print a 3 on a 7 by rotating it "spikes up", but
  you can't then print another rotated 3 on top of it, becasuse between the points of the 3
  it would overhang.

    Prompt Changes:
Prompt remains constant		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Same as typical promptView exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

So this test has 2 components, a visual one and a maths one.



The maths problem is computationally extreme - finding a prime
with no 3 diget sequence repeated. The answer is around 1000
digets long. If you tackle this first, you're going to have a bad time.



The visual problem is way simpler - each diget and orientation
choice limits the future choices, and the problem size drastically
shrinks with each step of it you solve. For example an 8:
    

  • Can't be printed on it's long or short side, as that has bridge overhangs.
    • 

  • When printed flat, must not occur after any number except 8 (or the start)
    • 



So... our answer set diverges into 4 immediately:
    

  • ^[0123456790]+
    • 

  • ^8[0123456790]+
    • 

  • ^88[0123456790]+
    • 

  • ^888[0123456790]+
    • 


The largest of which is the last, so the answer must start with 888, printed all flat.



Then if we put anything other than a 6 or a 9, we'll never be able to put a 6 or 9, therefore the next digit must be... etc etc.



(And we can interleave rotated 6 and 9s if we're careful not to make a 4-run of the same digit.) etc.

The visual part of the problem gives the prefix of the answer and greatly limits
the search space for the suffix. If the AI is 'offering to build a program to find the
prime number' or whatever, it probably deserves its 0, as an exhaustive search for this
required only ~50 calls to "isPrime" to discover the 24 digit answer.


AI Reasoning: Bottom-up stack that satisfies all listed constraints and the additional rules is the three-digit prime 331. Construction (ground up):
- Bottom digit: 3 printed flat on the build plate — broad, stable footprint.
- Middle digit: 3 printed rotated about the Y axis (rotate90Y) so its three horizontal segments become three upward contact points (three short spike-like high points). Those three high points simultaneously touch the low points of the middle 3 while keeping the center of gravity of the rotated 3 over the footprint of the flat 3 beneath it.
- Top digit: 1 printed rotated (rotate90Y) and balanced with its lowest contact point placed at the centroid where the three spike-tips of the rotated-3 meet (or on the trio so that all lowest points of the 1 contact the highest points of the supporting rotated 3). The 1’s center of gravity lies inside the supporting triangle/footprint provided by the rotated-3, and the entire stack’s center of gravity lies within the footprint of the bottom flat 3.

Why you cannot go higher:
- The only digits/orientations that produce multiple stable upward contact points usable to support another whole digit without bridging or cantilevers are the rotated-3 (three spikes) and the rotated-7 (single short/long spike). A rotated-3 cannot be stacked on top of another rotated-3 (the points between its spikes create unavoidable overhangs), and a rotated-7 supplies at most a single spike (insufficient to stably support many other 7-segment digits without overhang or out-of-footprint CG).
- Any attempt to extend the stack upward requires either repeating a supporting 3-pattern (which would create a repeated length-3 substring if done as a motif, and the problem forbids repeating any sequence of 3 more than once) or using unsupported single-spike contacts that violate the “all lowest points touch highest points” and no-cantilever constraints.
- Combinations that might appear to extend the stack (e.g., alternating a rotated-7 and a rotated-3) either produce overhangs when the next digit is placed, fail the center-of-gravity-within-support requirement, or necessarily form a forbidden 3-length repeated substring when extended to produce a larger number.

Therefore 331 is the largest prime that can be printed under the given 7-segment orientation/support rules and the repetition constraint.
    0.0000
Digit 3 (orientation: rotate90Y) has overhangs.

  

    





Ai suggested 331 (3 digits).
The correct answer is 24 digits long.


  

    Test 31: Prove a convex solid can fit throught itself
    Score: 0.00 / 3
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:


Here is a 3d object, specifically the 'unit cube', orientated such that its largest face is parallel
to the z=0 plane and centred at the origin.

I conject that this solid can fit through it's own projection if both carefully rotated.

Prove this conjecture by crafting two orientations:
- An orientation which when projected on Z creates a large shadow, being the 'hole'.
- An orientation which when projected on Z creates a small shadow, being the 'solid'.

The solid should be small enough to fit through the hole with a non-zero margin.

Provide each orientation as a 4 element quaternion.

If a solution is not possible using rotations around the origin, provide a 7 element transform:
4 element quaternion and a 3 element offset vector (in that order), the translation is applied in 3D after 
rotation but before projection.


    Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: Unit cube
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Can a dice fit through another dice, if they're both rotated optimally? This problem
was famously unsolved for decades.



This is now known and proven. Most convex solids can fit through themselves with room
to spare even, but calculate the orientations is non-trivial.

    Test 32: Essay section
    Score: 0.00 / 1
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

We are designing a file format for 3D data that's the result of a laser scan used by to
survey infastructure, buildings, streets, and other parts of modern life.

The laser scanner consists of two rotating mirrors, one high speed (600rpm) that sweeps
vertically, returns data from -80 degrees to + 80 degrees, and one slow speed that
rotates in arcs up to a full 360 degrees, and as slow as 1rph. 

The laser beam will impact objects in the scene and, for each beam, return an unknown
number of returns. 
- Laser beams that shoot off into the atmosphere do not return.
- Beams that hit a solid object respond with a signal value, representing the
  albedo and normal of the impacted surface.
- Beams can return multiple hits, when impacting surfaces like glass, water, fog,
  foilage, mirrors, dust, smoke, etc.

Image data is also captured in RGBA format using a spherical mirror mounted on the
same axis as the laser scanner, each 2x2 cell of beams contains 1 or more pixels
of RGB data.

Scanner position is fixed 90% of the time, but it can be mounted to a moving 
vechile for rapid data acquisition and the origin is kept updated via GPS.

A/D encoder resolution is 12 bits for returned beam signal strength, 16ghz for times,
and 20 bits for azimuth and elevation angles. Data loss within these limits is acceptable,
and desirable for compression. Data loss exceeding this limits is not desirable.

The user can set the capture rates based on their needs for speed vs quality. On board computer is quite powerful
and for the purpose of this exercise has infinite processing capability.

We're design a file format that can store this data as optimally as possible. Since the
file is immutable once written, care should be taken to ensure that the file is
as compact as possible. Optimising for typical use cases is what will help us get
an edge over the competition. Scan files can be expected to be 100s of millions of beams,
with 100s of billions not being unheard of.

Write a report suggesting all potential optimisations for this file format, both wise and
unwise, and justify your chocies for which to adopt.

    Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

This gets an LLM to write an essay or report, and then uses another LLM to grade it against
expected discoverys and insights. This relies on an LLM to be good enough to read an essay
and answer a simple question about it - most are good enough for this.

For each essay topic, the marking guide contains a list of 'good' and 'bad' ideas.
- For mentioning a 'good' idea, an LLM gets +0.5
- For recommending a 'good' idea, an LLM gets another +0.5.
- For mentioning a 'bad' idea and recommending it, an LLM gets - 1.
- For mentioning a 'bad' idea and recommending against it, an LLM gets +1
- Score is graded as a fraction out of len(good) + len(bad), clamped at 0.

    0.0000
NYI

  

    
NYI

  

    Test 33: Gravitational trickshots
    Score: 3.00 / 6
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

You are playing the role of God, building the solar system (inside an N-body physics simulator).

You previous chosen the mass of star: 10^30 kg. 

You previously chose the following planets: 
- 3 planets each 10^24 kg and ~15,000km in diameter (named Alice, Bob, and Carol)
- 2 planets each 10^25 kg and ~40,000km in diameter (named Dave and Eve)
- 2 planets each 10^26 kg and ~100,000km in diameter (named Frank and Grace)

The star's barycentre is the origin of the coordinate system. All coordinates are in km.
Consider a planet destroyed if it crosses the Roche limit of a larger body.

Lay out the star and 7 planets such that:

The system is stable for at least 100 years of simulated N-body physics.


    Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Gets the LLM to lay out planets in a solar system to achieve various orbital configurations,
starting with stable, and moving into complications like binary pair orbits, stability
for decades until sudden planet-into-sun collision, and at the top level, orbital slingshot
off one of the gas giants resulting in orbital reversal of the smallest planet.



AI Reasoning: I placed all seven planets on coplanar, prograde, near-circular orbits around the star at the origin (star mass = 1e30 kg). Semimajor axes increase by a constant factor (~1.6) so adjacent planets are widely separated (large multiples of their mutual Hill radii), avoiding close encounters and resonances on short timescales. Velocities are the circular-orbit speeds (v = sqrt(G*M_star / r)). With this spacing the orbital periods are short compared to 100 years (roughly 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.1, 8.4, 17.0 and 34.3 years respectively), and no planet comes close to the Roche limit of the star or of any larger planet. This layout is stable against close scattering and tidal disruption for at least 100 simulated years in a standard N-body integrator (assuming no large external perturbations).
    1.0000
System stable for 100 years

  

    


Orbital tracks over 100 years. White cubes = start positions, large spheres = final positions.


Colors: Alice, Bob, Carol, 
Dave, Eve, Frank, 
Grace, Sun (center)



  

    Subpass 1
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: I placed the star at the origin (M = 1e30 kg) and put each planet on an initially circular orbit about the star with a distinct orbital plane (different inclinations and ascending nodes) and different starting phase. Semi-major axes double successively (0.2 AU, 0.4 AU, …, 12.8 AU) to give very large inter-orbital separations; this makes mutual perturbations small and ensures mutual separations much larger than the planets' Hill spheres. Velocities are the circular orbital velocities v = sqrt(G*M_star / a) (G expressed in km^3 kg^-1 s^-2). I also picked starting true anomalies (phases) so they are not initially in conjunction. With this spacing and the chosen small-to-moderate inclinations, adjacent planets are separated by hundreds of millions of km (or more) and by many mutual Hill radii — well outside Roche-limit / collision danger — so the system is expected to remain dynamically stable for at least 200 years of N-body simulation (outermost orbital period ~65 years, so the outer planets complete several orbits in 200 years while staying well-separated). Numeric values given are positions (km) and velocities (km/s) in the star-centred inertial frame, computed from the circular-orbit orbital elements (a, i, Ω, f) described above. If you want, I can instead return classical orbital elements (a, e=0, i, Ω, ω=0, f) rather than Cartesian vectors.
    1.0000
System stable for 200 years

  

    


Orbital tracks over 200 years. White cubes = start positions, large spheres = final positions.


Colors: Alice, Bob, Carol, 
Dave, Eve, Frank, 
Grace, Sun (center)



  

    Subpass 2
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Design summary and timing estimate:
- I place seven planets on coplanar prograde Keplerian orbits around the central star (mass = 1e30 kg). Orbital radii are chosen to keep bodies well separated for many orbits, except for the pair Frank (mass 1e26 kg) and Grace (mass 1e26 kg). Frank and Grace have semi-major axes chosen so their synodic period ~ 53.8 years; initial angular offset is set so their first close conjunction (when Grace is at periastron and can cross Frank's orbit) happens at about 54 years. That yields no impacts for at least 50 years and produces at least one impact before year 60.
- Key numbers and logic: mean motions n = sqrt(mu / a^3) with mu = G*Mstar = 6.6743e10 km^3/s^2. I set Frank: a_F = 1.5000e8 km (1 AU), Grace: a_G = 1.5258e8 km and e_G = 0.0169 so Grace's periastron drops inside Frank's orbit. The angular offset between their initial mean longitudes is Delta_lambda0 = 10 degrees = 0.1745329 rad (Grace ahead of Frank). The conjunction time estimate (first future conjunction where longitudes align) is t_conj ≈ (2π - Delta_lambda0) / (n_F - n_G) ≈ 1.697e9 s ≈ 53.8 years. At that conjunction Grace will be near periastron and the two orbits intersect — causing a collision before year 60.
- I spaced the other five planets (Alice, Bob, Carol, Dave, Eve) on concentric, non-crossing circular orbits (different semi-major axes and initial longitudes) so no close approaches or orbit crossings occur with Frank/Grace, and they remain safely separated (tens of millions of km) during the first decades.
- All positions are given in km and velocities in km/s, relative to the system barycentre (star fixed at origin). Orbits are coplanar (z=0). These initial conditions were computed from simple Keplerian relations (circular for e=0 bodies; standard position/velocity from true anomaly for the slightly eccentric Grace). The Frank-Grace encounter timing is approximate but chosen to meet the requirement: no impacts for >=50 years, an impact before 60 years (expected ≈54 years).
    0.0000
No impact occurred between year 50 and 60

  

    


Orbital tracks over 60 years. White cubes = start positions, large spheres = final positions.


Colors: Alice, Bob, Carol, 
Dave, Eve, Frank, 
Grace, Sun (center)



  

    Subpass 3
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: I place all bodies coplanar, star at origin (given M = 1e30 kg). Six planets are on near-circular, well-separated prograde orbits (so they remain long-term stable). One planet (Alice) is the inner planet and is deliberately given a small perturbation and placed near a 2:1 mean-motion relationship with a much more massive nearby planet (Frank). That resonance with Frank (mass 1e26 kg) will preferentially pump Alice's eccentricity while the other planets, being more distant and on non-resonant, well-spaced orbits, remain stable. In a purely conservative N-body integrator without tides or drag, exact timing of a collision is sensitive to phases; to ensure Alice completes many orbits before hitting the star, I give it a short orbital period (<< 1 yr) so it will complete at least 100 revolutions within 100 years. The small initial radial velocity and the resonance seed will drive chaotic eccentricity growth so Alice is the only body expected to cross the star's Roche/collision limit after many orbits. Note: a strict guarantee of a collision exactly after N orbits requires non-gravitational dissipation or carefully tuned chaotic phasing; the configuration below isolates the instability to Alice while keeping the others stable, and in practice this setup causes Alice's eccentricity to grow and lead to star-impact on the required timescale in typical N-body runs (or immediately if you add a small dissipative term).

    Subpass 4
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output

AI Reasoning: I place all planets on near-circular, coplanar orbits about the star at the origin. Frank and Grace form an equal-mass binary (each 1e26 kg) whose center-of-mass orbits the star at 1.0e9 km (~6.69 AU). The binary semi-major axis (separation) is 5.0e6 km (so each is 2.5e6 km from the binary COM). Their mutual circular orbital speed about the COM is ≈0.817 km/s each (mutual relative speed ≈1.634 km/s), and the COM orbital speed about the star at 1.0e9 km is ≈8.17 km/s. I set the binary separation perpendicular to the COM orbital motion so the two bodies each get the COM tangential velocity plus/minus their mutual velocity. With a 5e6 km separation they remain well inside the 10e6 km requirement (separation = 5e6 km constant), so they will spend the entire simulation time (≧100 years) within 10 million km of each other. I checked tidal/Hill constraints: the binary Hill radius about the star at 1e9 km is ≈4.05e7 km (≫ 5e6 km), and the mutual Roche limit (equal density, fluid assumption) is ≈1.23e5 km ≪ 5e6 km, so the pair is not tidally disrupted by each other or by the star on these circular orbits.

All planets are given positions in km and velocities in km/s. Orbits are arranged to be circular about the star (velocities are tangential) except Frank and Grace include the mutual orbital velocity about their COM added to the COM orbital velocity. This layout yields a stable configuration where Frank and Grace are a long-lived binary pair that remain within 10 million km of each other for well over 100 years.

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

AI Reasoning: Plan: put Alice on a relatively wide orbit so its orbital speed around the 1e30-kg star is modest (~11.6 km/s) so a close encounter with one of the much-more-massive planets (Frank or Grace, 1e26 kg) can plausibly deliver a comparable impulse and flip the sign of Alice's orbital angular momentum about the star. I set Alice at ~5e8 km from the star (period ~8.6 years) and initially moving clockwise (negative Lz). Frank is placed interior and given a slightly super-circular speed and a small inclination so its orbit will intersect Alice's orbit within a couple of Alice orbital periods; a close (but not Roche-crossing) encounter with Frank can deliver an order-10 km/s impulse that is large enough to reverse Alice's ~11.6 km/s orbital velocity. That produces the required behaviour: Alice completes at least two clockwise orbits (≈17 years) before the planned encounter, then is flipped and completes at least two counterclockwise orbits within 50 years. I keep all periapses and planned close-approach distances comfortably larger than the (approximate) Roche-radius scale for the larger planets (we target minimum encounter distances ~several 1e5 km, while giant-planet radii are O(1e5) km). The star sits at the origin with zero velocity (star barycentre = origin). The numbers below are a practical initial-condition set that is physically plausible for the described scenario; exact encounter timing will depend on the N-body integrator and small changes to the initial phasing. If you want I can refine the phasing to move the encounter earlier or later, or increase/decrease the planned closest approach distance to make the flip more/less robust.

    Test 34: Net Folding - Can a 2D net fold into a 3D polyhedron?
    Score: 2.52 / 7
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

A 'net' is a 2D pattern of connected polygons that can be folded along shared edges to form a 3D polyhedron.

Given the following 2D net laid out on the XY plane with Z=0:


Face A: Square at (0,0) to (1,1) - this is the BASE (bottom)
Face B: Square at (1,0) to (2,1) - connected to A on the right (east side of A)
Face C: Square at (2,0) to (3,1) - connected to B on the right
Face D: Square at (0,1) to (1,2) - connected to A on the top (north side of A)
Face E: Square at (1,1) to (2,2) - connected to B on top AND D on right
Face F: Square at (1,2) to (2,3) - connected to E on top



Each face is labeled with a letter. Edges where faces connect are fold lines.

Your task is to specify the final 3D position and orientation of each face after folding the net into a closed 3D shape.

For each face, provide:
- A 3D position (the centroid of the face after folding)
- A normal vector (pointing outward from the closed polyhedron)

The base face 'A' remains fixed at its original position on Z=0 with normal pointing down (-Z).

Rules:
- Adjacent faces in the net must remain connected at their shared edge after folding
- The result must form a closed, watertight polyhedron
- All normals must point outward

    Prompt Changes:
Different polyhedra nets of increasing complexity		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: Cube (cross net)
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Can an LLM mentally fold a 2D net into a 3D polyhedron?



This is a classic spatial reasoning test used in IQ assessments. The LLM must:

    

  • Understand how 2D faces connect at fold lines
    • 

  • Visualize the 3D result of folding operations
    • 

  • Calculate correct positions and orientations for each face
    • 


Starting with simple cube nets and progressing to more complex polyhedra.


AI Reasoning: The given 6 squares form one of the standard cube nets. Folding the net up along the indicated edges produces a unit cube (side length = 1) with face A fixed as the bottom (Z=0) face. I assign the other five faces to the remaining cube faces so that all original hinge connections are respected (B hinged to A, C hinged to B, D hinged to A, E hinged to B and D, F hinged to E) and the result is a closed, watertight cube. The coordinates below use the original A square as spanning x∈[0,1], y∈[0,1], z=0; after folding each face lies on the expected cube face plane. Normals are outward-pointing unit vectors.

Parameters: Tetrahedron net
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Fold the three outer triangles up so their free vertices meet at the single apex of the regular tetrahedron. The three base vertices remain at the given XY coordinates (z=0). The apex is located above the base centroid at height z = sqrt(8/3) so that its distance to each base vertex equals the side length 2. Each face centroid is the average of its three vertex coordinates. Each face normal is computed as the outward unit normal (cross product of two edge vectors with sign chosen to point away from the polyhedron interior). Face A stays fixed in the XY plane with its normal pointing down (-Z).

AI Reasoning: The 8-triangle net folds to a regular octahedron (8 equilateral faces, side length 1). I placed the octahedron so that face A (the bottom triangle in the net) lies in the plane Z=0 with outward normal (0,0,-1) and centroid at Z=0 (as requested). I labeled the octahedron faces by the ± sign-triples s=(s1,s2,s3) (s_i = ±1) as follows, which matches the net adjacency: A=(-,-,-), B=(+,-,-), C=(+,+,-), H=(-,+,-) along the bottom row, and D,E,F,G the corresponding top-row faces with z-sign flipped: D=(-,-,+), E=(+,-,+), F=(+,+,+), G=(-,+,+). For a regular octahedron with side length 1 the face-centroid distance from the solid center is c = 1/√6. I rotated the octahedron so that the cube-diagonal (1,1,1)/√3 maps to +Z (hence (-1,-1,-1)/√3 maps to -Z), then translated the whole solid up by c so A’s centroid lies at Z=0. Using that rotation+translation gives closed-form coordinates; numerically the centroids and outward unit normals are below (components rounded to 10 significant digits).

Before I compute explicit numeric centroids and outward normals I need to confirm the format and precision you want: do you want (a) an exact symbolic description in terms of the golden ratio φ = (1+√5)/2 and simple algebraic expressions, or (b) floating-point coordinates (decimal) for the centroid and unit normal of each of the 20 faces (with e.g. 6–8 significant digits)? If you prefer, I will (by default) place the centroid of face A at (0,0,0), its plane coincident with Z=0, and return 20 decimal centroids and outward unit normals (6 decimals). Please confirm which option you want and I will produce the full list.

AI Reasoning: The provided net is the net of a regular dodecahedron (12 regular pentagonal faces). When folded into the closed polyhedron the 12 face-centroids lie on the directions of the 12 vertices of an icosahedron (the dodecahedron–icosahedron dual). I fix face A so its centroid is at (0,0,-r) on the plane Z=0 and its outward normal is (0,0,-1). For edge length a = 1 the distance from the dodecahedron center to a face (face-centroid radius, i.e. the dodecahedron inradius) is r ≈ 1.113516364411. The 12 outward unit normals (directions) are the 12 rotated/normalized icosahedron-vertex directions below; each face centroid = r * (its unit normal). The list below assigns A–L to one convenient consistent labeling of the 12 face-centroid directions (A fixed to the downward pole). All normals are unit length and point outward; adjacent faces in the net become adjacent faces of the dodecahedron and share the corresponding edges of the folded polyhedron.

Parameters: Truncated Tetrahedron net (8 faces) - HARD
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: The 2D net is the uniform truncated tetrahedron: 4 equilateral-triangle faces and 4 regular-hexagon faces, all edges length 1. I construct the 3D shape by truncating a regular tetrahedron of edge length 3 (uniform truncation parameter 1/3 gives all new edges length 1). The truncated-tetrahedron geometry (face centroids and face normals) is computed from that model, then rigidly rotated + translated so that the triangular face corresponding to label A lies in the Z=0 plane with its outward normal pointing down (0,0,-1) and its centroid at (0,0,0) as requested. The other faces follow by the same rigid motion. All normals given are unit-length and point outward from the closed polyhedron. (Labels: triangles A,B,C,D correspond to the 4 truncated-vertex triangular faces; hexagons E,F,G,H correspond to the 4 truncated original faces opposite those vertices.)

You are analyzing a gear train system with special mechanical components.

Gear 1: 13 teeth, (input gear), meshes with Gear 2

Gear 48: 11 teeth, meshes with Gears 47, 49
Gear 49: 61 teeth, meshes with Gears 48, 50
Gear 50: 83 teeth, meshes with Gear 49

The input gear (gear 1) is rotating CLOCKWISE at 1000 RPM.


Standard gear rules apply:
- Meshing gears rotate in opposite directions
- Speed ratio = driving gear teeth / driven gear teeth
- Gears on the same shaft rotate together (same speed and direction)


For each gear in the system, determine:
1. Its rotation direction (clockwise, counterclockwise, or stopped)
2. Its rotation speed in RPM (use 0 if stopped, use average RPM for variable-speed gears)
3. Any special notes about the gear's behavior

Important: Some gears may be stopped (0 RPM) due to clutches, ratchets, or shattered components.

    Prompt Changes:
Progressively more complex gear trains with special components		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: 50-gear linear chain
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Tests mechanical reasoning about complex gear trains with special components.



Key concepts:

    

  • Meshing gears rotate in opposite directions
    • 

  • Speed ratio = driving gear teeth / driven gear teeth
    • 

  • Gears on the same shaft rotate together
    • 

  • Chain drives preserve rotation direction (unlike meshing)
    • 

  • Clutches can disconnect parts of the drive train
    • 

  • Ratchets only allow rotation in one direction
    • 

  • Fragile gears shatter above certain RPM thresholds
    • 


This tests whether an LLM can trace cause-and-effect through massive mechanical systems
with up to 200 gears and special complications.


AI Reasoning: Method: I treated the train as a single serial chain of meshing gears with no fixed shafts specified (so no gear pairs are rigidly locked to each other). For two meshing gears: direction flips and rpm_driven = rpm_driver * (teeth_driver / teeth_driven). In a pure linear chain this telescopes, so gear n's speed magnitude = input_rpm * (teeth_1 / teeth_n) = 1000 * (13 / teeth_n). Direction alternates each mesh, so gear 1 (input) is CW, gear 2 is CCW, gear 3 CW, etc. No gear in the provided data was indicated as stopped (no clutches/ratchets/shattered parts specified), so all gears rotate at the values below. Special notes summary: • rpm_n = 13000 / teeth_n. • Direction = CW for odd-numbered gears, CCW for even-numbered gears. • Several gears with fewer teeth than the input (13) spin faster than the input (e.g., gears with 11 or 19 teeth).

50/50 gears correct

No gears were specified as clutched, ratcheted, or shattered, so none are treated as stopped. I give RPMs rounded to three decimals. (If you want exact rational values I can provide them.)

    Subpass 2
Parameters: 5-way branching system
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Approach: I treated gear 1 as the input (given: clockwise, 3000 RPM) and propagated direction (meshing flips direction) and speed (rpm_driven = rpm_driver * teeth_driver / teeth_driven) along each simple chain. Where the mesh network contains odd closed loops that would create an impossible parity/velocity constraint if all gears were rigidly coupled, I assumed those gears are disengaged/locked (0 RPM) due to clutches/ratchets/shattered components as noted in the problem statement. In particular, the cluster of gears {2,3,4,5} together with gear 1 contains multiple 3-gear loops (odd cycles: 1-2-3-1, 1-3-4-1, 1-4-5-1). Those loops produce incompatible direction constraints if they were all free, so I treat gears 2–5 as stopped (0 RPM) / isolated from gear 1 (e.g., clutch disengaged or broken). All other branches (through gears 6–9, 10–13, 14–17, 18–21) are simple chains with no parity contradictions, so their directions and speeds are computed directly. Values given numeric (rounded to 4 decimal places where repeating). Special notes: gears 2–5 are stopped (0 RPM) to resolve impossible odd-cycle constraints; gear 1 is the driving input (CW, 3000 RPM).

No information was given about clutches/ratchets/shattered components, so I assume none are stopped. Below I list shaft groupings, computed speeds, and key mesh-driven relationships used: 

Shaft A (Gears 1,2,3,4,5): 2400 RPM clockwise (input). Gear 2 (20t) drives Gear 9 (15t).
Shaft B (Gears 6,7,8,9,10): 3200 RPM counterclockwise (Gear9 = 3200 CCW). Gear 9 (15t) drives Gear 14 (55t).
Shaft C (Gears 11,12,13,14,15): 872.727... RPM clockwise (3200*(15/55)). Gear 14 (55t) drives Gear 19 (25t).
Shaft D (Gears 16,17,18,19,20): 1920 RPM counterclockwise (872.727*(55/25) = 1920). Gear 17 (25t) drives Gear 22 (55t).
Shaft E (Gears 21,22,23,24,25): 872.727... RPM clockwise (1920*(25/55) = 9600/11 ≈ 872.727). Gear 24 (45t) drives Gear 27 (20t).

Shaft H (Gears 36,37,38,39,40): 2036.364... RPM counterclockwise (28800/11*(35/45) = 22400/11 ≈ 2036.364).

All gears on the same shaft share the shaft speed and direction. No gears were indicated as stopped in the data provided.

    Subpass 5
Parameters: 25-gear system with clutch
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Method: Starting from Gear 1 (500 RPM clockwise), each meshing reverses direction and multiplies speed by (driving teeth / driven teeth). Because the teeth counts along the chain telescope, every gear that is mechanically connected to Gear 1 (i.e., gears 1–9) has omega = 500 * 53 / (teeth of that gear) = 26500 / (teeth). The clutch at Gear 10 is disengaged, so Gear 10 and all downstream gears (11–25) are disconnected and at 0 RPM. Notes: directions alternate each mesh; gears 10–25 are stopped due to the disengaged clutch. Also note some equalities: Gear 5 and Gear 9 have the same teeth (11) and thus the same RPM (26500/11 ≈ 2409.091 RPM). Gear 6 and Gear 10 would have the same theoretical RPM (26500/31 ≈ 854.839 RPM) if the clutch were engaged, but Gear 10 is stopped here.

Parameters: 20-gear system with ratchets
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Assumptions and method:
- Meshing gears reverse direction. Speed ratio = driver_teeth / driven_teeth. Gears on same shaft rotate together.
- I interpret “forward” = counterclockwise (CCW) and “reverse” = clockwise (CW). With that mapping the two ratchets do NOT block the naturally implied rotation of the train (see notes below), so no gear is forced to 0 RPM by a ratchet. If you want the opposite mapping, say so and I will recompute.
- I propagated from Gear 1 (input) using exact tooth ratios; results are given rounded to three decimal places. No clutches/shattered parts were stated beyond the two ratchets; because with the assumed mapping both ratchets permit the computed motion, no gear is stopped.
Notes about special components:
- Gear 8 has a ratchet that allows forward rotation (assumed = CCW). The computed motion for Gear 8 is CCW at 800 RPM, so the ratchet is allowing motion and does not block.
- Gear 15 has a ratchet that allows reverse rotation (assumed = CW). The computed motion for Gear 15 is CW at 241.860 RPM, so that ratchet is allowing motion and does not block.
- Several intermediate gear ratios produce recurrence to the input speed (800 RPM) at gears where the multiplied tooth ratios cancel; those are listed in the gear table.
If you want a different convention for “forward/reverse” or want me to treat a blocked ratchet as preventing motion upstream, tell me which convention and I will recompute which gears stop.

Parameters: 16-gear system with fragile components
View exact prompt

AI Reasoning: Method: propagate rotation from input (Gear 1: 50,000 RPM clockwise) through each meshing pair using: opposite rotation on mesh, speed ratio = driving teeth / driven teeth, and identical rpm/direction for gears on the same shaft. Apply fragile rule: a fragile gear shatters if its RPM > threshold; if it shatters, that gear and downstream gears stop (set to 0 RPM). Results below show direction, computed RPM (rounded to two decimals), and special notes where applicable. No fragile gear exceeded its threshold, so no gear shattered and no gear stopped.

No stoppages, clutches, ratchets or shattered components were specified, so all gears are assumed to rotate. - If you want any values in exact rational form rather than decimal, I can provide them.

Parameters: Complex system with multiple complications
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Method and key rules applied:
- Meshing gears rotate in opposite directions.
- RPM_driven = RPM_driver * (teeth_driver / teeth_driven).
- Clutch (gear 8) is disengaged ⇒ gear 8 and everything downstream from it (gears 9–13) are disconnected and therefore 0 RPM.
- Fragile (gear 20) shatters if RPM > 1000. Gear 20, driven by gear 1, would exceed 1000 RPM and therefore shatters; gear 20 and everything downstream from it (gears 21–25) stop (0 RPM).
- Ratchet (gear 12) allows rotation in one direction only; however that branch is disconnected by the clutch, so gear 12 remains stopped. If that branch were driven, the ratchet could block motion if the rotation were in the forbidden direction.

Summary of special conditions encountered:
- Clutch at gear 8: disengaged → gears 8–13 are 0 RPM.
- Fragile gear 20: input would force gear 20 to 9000 RPM (>1000) → gear 20 shatters and stops; gears 21–25 (downstream) stop.
- Ratchet at gear 12: branch is disconnected by the clutch, so it stays stopped; note it would permit only one rotation direction if driven.

Calculated gear directions and speeds (see gear list for per-gear notes).

Typical Prompt:

You are given a 3D object and a cutting plane. Determine the 2D shape that results from 
slicing the object with that plane.


A unit cube with corners at (0,0,0) and (1,1,1).



Cutting plane: Z = 0.5 (horizontal slice through the middle)
U-axis: X direction, V-axis: Y direction


Describe the resulting 2D cross-section shape by providing its vertices in order (clockwise 
when viewed from the positive side of the cutting plane's normal).

Express all coordinates in the local 2D coordinate system of the cutting plane, where:
- The origin is at the projection of the origin onto the cutting plane
- U-axis and V-axis are as specified for each plane

Different 3D objects and cutting planes		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: Cube horizontal slice
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Tests the ability to mentally slice 3D objects and predict the resulting 2D cross-section.



This is a fundamental spatial reasoning skill:

    

  • Horizontal slices through regular solids
    • 

  • Diagonal/angled cuts
    • 

  • Understanding how circles become ellipses when cylinders are cut at angles
    • 

  • Recognizing that cube diagonals create rectangles
    • 


Used in medical imaging, manufacturing, and architecture.


AI Reasoning: The plane z=0.5 intersects the unit cube in the horizontal square with corners where x and y are 0 or 1 at z=0.5: (0,0,0.5),(1,0,0.5),(1,1,0.5),(0,1,0.5). The projection of the origin onto the plane is (0,0,0.5), so local (u,v) = (x,y). Viewed from the positive normal (positive z), a clockwise ordering is (0,0) -> (0,1) -> (1,1) -> (1,0).

Parameters: Cylinder angled slice
View exact prompt

AI Reasoning: Plane: y+z-5=0 (normal (0,1,1)). Projecting the origin onto the plane gives plane-origin at (0,2.5,2.5). Plane in-plane basis: U = (1,0,0) (given), V = (0,1,-1)/√2 (unit vector in the plane orthogonal to U). For a point P=(x,y,z) in the plane coordinates u = (P - plane-origin)·U = x, v = (P - plane-origin)·V = (y-z)/√2. The cylinder gives x^2 + y^2 = 4 and plane gives z = 5 - y. Eliminating x,z gives an ellipse in (u,v): u^2 + ((v√2 +5)/2)^2 = 4, which simplifies to (u^2)/4 + ((v + 5/√2)^2)/8 = 1. Thus the intersection is an ellipse centered at (u,v) = (0, -5/√2) with semi-axes lengths 2 (along the u/X direction) and 2√2 (along the v direction). The full ellipse lies within the cylinder height z∈[0,10] (z = 5 - 2 sinθ ranges [3,7]), so no truncation by the end caps occurs.

Parameters: Torus horizontal slice - HARD
View exact prompt
View raw AI output

AI Reasoning: Slice plane z=1 intersects the torus where (sqrt(x^2+y^2)-R)^2 + z^2 = r^2. For fixed z the radial distance s = sqrt(x^2+y^2) satisfies (s - R)^2 = r^2 - z^2. With R=5, r=2, z=1 we get r^2 - z^2 = 4-1 = 3, so s = R ± sqrt(3) = 5 ± √3. Thus the intersection in the plane z=1 is two concentric circles (an annulus) centered at the projection of the origin onto the plane (which is the local 2D origin). In the local (u,v) coordinates (u = x, v = y) the center is (0,0) and the outer and inner radii are 5+√3 and 5-√3 (numerically ~6.7320508076 and ~3.2679491924). Vertices list is empty because the shape is circular; ordering clockwise from the positive side of the plane normal corresponds to decreasing polar angle.

AI Reasoning: The intersection of the given right circular cone (apex at (0,0,10), base radius 5 at z=0) with the plane 0.5 y + 0.866(z-5)=0 is an ellipse. Set the plane as z = 5 - (1/√3) y (since tan30° = 1/√3). Substituting into the cone equation x^2 + y^2 = (0.5(10 - z))^2 gives the planar conic
x^2 + (11/12) y^2 - (5/(2√3)) y - 6.25 = 0.
Completing the square yields
x^2 + (11/12)(y - y0)^2 = 75/11,
with y0 = (15/(11√3)). The plane-local coordinates are chosen so u is the X direction and v is the in-plane slope direction v̂ = (0,√3/2,-1/2). The plane origin is the orthogonal projection of (0,0,0) onto the plane; expressing the ellipse center relative to that plane-origin and projecting onto (u,v) gives center (u=0, v = -35/22). In these local (u,v) coordinates the ellipse axes are aligned with u and v, with semi-axes
u_semi = sqrt(75/11) = (5√33)/11 ≈ 2.6111648393 (along +/− u)
v_semi = sqrt(1200/121) = (20√3)/11 ≈ 3.1491832880 (along +/− v).
(All intermediate algebra is in the reasoning above; numerics rounded to 10 significant digits.)

Parameters: Intersecting cylinders slice - HARD

AI Reasoning: Cylinder A (axis along Z) gives x^2 + y^2 <= 9 in the plane z=2. Cylinder B (axis along X) gives y^2 + z^2 <= 9, so at z=2 we have y^2 <= 9-4 = 5, i.e. |y| <= sqrt(5). Thus the slice is the part of the disk x^2 + y^2 <= 9 with y between -sqrt(5) and +sqrt(5). The boundary consists of the two horizontal line segments y = ±sqrt(5) between the circle intersections and the two circular arcs x^2 + y^2 = 9 connecting those endpoints. The circle/line intersection satisfies x^2 = 9 - y^2 = 4, so x = ±2. Taking the view from +z (so +x to the right, +y up) and listing vertices in clockwise order gives: (2, +sqrt(5)) -> (2, -sqrt(5)) -> (-2, -sqrt(5)) -> (-2, +sqrt(5)).

    Test 37: Mental Rotation - Identify which 3D shapes are rotations of each other
    Score: 7.36 / 8
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

You are shown multiple 3D objects made of connected unit cubes (like 3D Tetris pieces).
Each object is described by listing the (x, y, z) coordinates of its unit cubes.

Your task is to identify which objects are the SAME shape, just rotated differently in 3D space.
Two shapes are considered the same if one can be rotated (but NOT reflected/mirrored) to match the other.

Objects to analyze:


Object 1: cubes at (0,5,5), (0,6,5), (0,5,4), (0,6,4)
Object 2: cubes at (4,1,5), (4,1,4), (4,0,5), (4,0,4)
Object 3: cubes at (0,1,5), (-1,1,5), (-2,1,5), (-2,1,6)
Object 4: cubes at (0,3,0), (1,3,0), (2,3,0), (2,3,-1)

Group the objects by their underlying shape. Return groups where each group contains 
object numbers that are rotations of each other.

    Prompt Changes:
Increasing number of objects and unique shapes		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: 4 objects, 2 unique shapes
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Mental rotation is a core spatial reasoning ability - recognizing that two objects are 
the same shape despite being shown from different orientations.



This test presents multiple 3D polycube shapes and asks the LLM to group those that are 
rotations of each other (but NOT mirror images).



Key challenges:

    

  • Mentally rotating 3D coordinates
    • 

  • Distinguishing rotations from reflections (chiral shapes)
    • 

  • Handling multiple comparison groups simultaneously
    • 


This mirrors classic spatial ability tests used in psychology research.


AI Reasoning: Objects 1 and 2 are both 2x2 flat square blocks (four cubes forming a 2-by-2 square). Objects 3 and 4 are both the same L-shaped tetromino: three cubes in a line with the fourth attached at one end, just rotated. Thus the shapes group as [1,2] and [3,4].
    1.0000

    Test 38: Balance and Center of Mass - Will it tip over?
    Score: 7.70 / 10
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

You are analyzing the stability of stacked objects. Each object has a mass and occupies a 
rectangular region in 3D space.


Object 1: Mass 10 kg, occupies region X:[0,4], Y:[0,4], Z:[0,2] (a 4x4x2 block on ground)
Object 2: Mass 5 kg, occupies region X:[1,3], Y:[1,3], Z:[2,4] (a 2x2x2 block centered on top)


The bottom object rests on a flat surface (the ground). Objects are stacked on top of each other.
Gravity pulls downward (-Z direction).

Determine:
1. Is the entire stack stable?
2. If not, which object tips first (from bottom to top)?
3. What is the location of the combined center of mass of the entire stack?

Assume uniform density for all objects (center of mass = geometric center).

    Prompt Changes:
Various stacking configurations testing stability		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: Simple centered stack
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Tests understanding of physical stability and center of mass.



Key physics concepts:

    

  • Center of mass is at the geometric center for uniform density objects
    • 

  • Combined CoM is the weighted average of individual CoMs
    • 

  • A stack tips when combined CoM falls outside the support footprint
    • 

  • Heavier objects on top have larger effect on combined CoM
    • 


This is crucial for robotics, construction, and any physical manipulation task.


AI Reasoning: Object centers: Obj1 at (2,2,1), Obj2 at (2,2,3). Combined COM = (10*2 + 5*2)/15, (10*2 +5*2)/15, (10*1 +5*3)/15 = (2, 2, 25/15) = (2, 2, 5/3 ≈ 1.6667). The projection of the combined COM onto the ground is (2,2), which lies inside the bottom object's base footprint X:[0,4],Y:[0,4]. Also the top object's own COM projection (2,2) lies inside its contact/support region on the top face of object 1 (which covers at least X:[1,3],Y:[1,3]). Therefore no object will tip.
    1.0000
Stability correct: True
CoM correct: [2, 2, 1.6666666666666667]

  

    
Problem:

Object 1: Mass 10 kg, occupies region X:[0,4], Y:[0,4], Z:[0,2] (a 4x4x2 block on ground)
Object 2: Mass 5 kg, occupies region X:[1,3], Y:[1,3], Z:[2,4] (a 2x2x2 block centered on top)


Your answer:
Is Stable: True
Tipping Object: 0
Center of Mass: [2, 2, 1.6666666666666667]

Expected:
Is Stable: True
Tipping Object: None
Center of Mass: [2.0, 2.0, 1.67]

  

    Subpass 1
Parameters: Offset but stable
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Compute centers: Object1 center = (3,2,0.5), mass 20. Object2 center = (5,2,2), mass 5. Combined center of mass = (20*(3,2,0.5)+5*(5,2,2))/25 = (85/25,50/25,20/25) = (3.4,2,0.8). Projected COM on ground = (3.4,2). Bottom object's support footprint on the ground is X:[0,6], Y:[0,4]; (3.4,2) lies inside that rectangle. The top object's contact footprint on the lower object is X:[4,6], Y:[1,3]; the top object's own center (5,2) lies inside its contact footprint. Therefore neither object is out beyond its support edge and the stack is stable.
    1.0000
Stability correct: True
CoM correct: [3.4, 2, 0.8]

  

    
Problem:

Object 1: Mass 20 kg, occupies region X:[0,6], Y:[0,4], Z:[0,1] (a 6x4x1 base plate)
Object 2: Mass 5 kg, occupies region X:[4,6], Y:[1,3], Z:[1,3] (a 2x2x2 block offset to one side)


Your answer:
Is Stable: True
Tipping Object: 0
Center of Mass: [3.4, 2, 0.8]

Expected:
Is Stable: True
Tipping Object: None
Center of Mass: [3.4, 2.0, 0.8]

  

    Subpass 2
Parameters: Overhang - will tip
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Compute centers: Obj1 center at (1,1,1) mass 5 kg. Obj2 center at (3,1,2.5) mass 10 kg. Combined COM = (5*1 + 10*3)/15, (5*1 + 10*1)/15, (5*1 + 10*2.5)/15 = (7/3, 1, 2) ≈ (2.3333, 1, 2).

Stability test: an object (or stack) is stable if the vertical projection of its center of mass lies inside the support polygon of the surface contacting it. Obj2 is supported by the overlap with Obj1: X overlap is [1,2], Y overlap [0,2]. Obj2’s projected x = 3, which lies outside [1,2] → Obj2 alone is unstable on Obj1 and will tip about the support edge at x = 2. The entire stack’s combined COM projected x = 2.3333 which lies outside the bottom block’s base X = [0,2] → the whole stack is not stable on the ground either. Which tips first: Obj2’s COM is 1.0 m beyond its support edge (3.0 − 2.0 = 1.0 m), while the combined COM is only 0.3333 m beyond the bottom base edge (2.3333 − 2.0 = 0.3333 m). Therefore the top object (Obj2) will tip first about the top-block edge, before the bottom block would tip as a whole.

Stability correct: False
Tipping object wrong: got 2, expected 1
CoM correct: [2.3333333333333335, 1, 2]

    Test 39: Collision Prediction - Will moving objects collide?
    Score: 10.00 / 10
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

Multiple objects are moving through 3D space. Each object is a sphere with a given radius,
starting position, and constant velocity.


Object 1: Sphere with radius 1m at (0, 0, 0), moving with velocity (10, 0, 0) m/s
Object 2: Sphere with radius 1m at (100, 0, 0), moving with velocity (-10, 0, 0) m/s


Assuming all objects continue moving in straight lines at constant speed:

1. Will any pair of objects collide? (Collision = their surfaces touch or overlap)
2. If yes, which pair collides first?
3. At what time does the first collision occur?
4. Where (XYZ coordinates) does the first collision happen?

Time starts at t=0. Positions are in meters, velocities in meters/second.

    Prompt Changes:
Various collision scenarios in 3D		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: Head-on collision
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Tests ability to predict collisions between moving objects in 3D space.



Key concepts:

    

  • Relative motion between objects
    • 

  • Solving quadratic equations for collision time
    • 

  • Understanding near misses vs actual collisions
    • 

  • Finding earliest collision among multiple pairs
    • 


This is fundamental for physics simulations, games, robotics, and autonomous navigation.


AI Reasoning: Initial center separation = 100 m along x. Closing speed = |v1 - v2| = |(10,0,0) - (-10,0,0)| = 20 m/s. Collision occurs when center separation = r1 + r2 = 2 m. Solve 100 - 20 t = 2 => t = (100 - 2)/20 = 98/20 = 4.9 s. At that time p1 = (10*4.9,0,0) = (49,0,0) and p2 = (100 - 10*4.9,0,0) = (51,0,0). The contact point (midpoint between centers for equal radii) is (50,0,0).
    1.0000
Collision prediction correct: True
Colliding pair correct: [1, 2]
Collision time correct: 4.900s
Collision point correct: [50, 0, 0]

Will Collide: True
Colliding Pair: [1, 2]
Collision Time: 4.9
Collision Point: [50, 0, 0]

Expected:

Colliding Pair: [1, 2]
Collision Time: 4.900s
Collision Point: [50.0, 0.0, 0.0]

Legend:
● Red = Colliding pair (first)
● Yellow = Will collide (later)
● Green = Safe (no collision)

    Subpass 1
Parameters: Near miss
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Relative position r0 = (50,3,0). Relative velocity v = v2−v1 = (-20,0,0). The center-to-center vector is r(t) = (50−20t, 3, 0). Distance squared D^2(t) = (50−20t)^2 + 3^2. The spheres collide when D^2(t) = (2)^2 = 4. But (50−20t)^2 + 9 = 4 implies (50−20t)^2 = −5, which has no real solution. The minimum separation occurs when the x-component is zero (t = 50/20 = 2.5 s), giving separation = 3 m > 2 m, so they never touch.

Parameters: Three objects - one collision
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Compute pairwise relative motion and solve for times when center-to-center distance = sum of radii = 4 m.

Pair (1,2):
- r0 = p2-p1 = (100,0,0). v = v2-v1 = (-10,0,0).
- Distance(t) = |100 - 10 t|. Solve |100 - 10 t| = 4 => 100 - 10 t = 4 => t = 9.6 s (the earlier root).
- Positions at t=9.6: p1 = (5*9.6,5*9.6,0) = (48,48,0), p2 = (100-5*9.6,5*9.6,0) = (52,48,0).
- Contact point on the line between centers (2 m from each center) is (50,48,0).

Pair (1,3):
- r0 = (50,200,0). v = (-5,-15,0). Solve |r0+v t|^2 = 16 gives quadratic 250 t^2 - 6500 t + 42484 = 0 with negative discriminant => no real solution → no collision.

Pair (2,3):
- r0 = (-50,200,0). v = (5,-15,0). Same quadratic arises, discriminant < 0 => no collision.

Therefore only objects 1 and 2 collide, and they do so first at t = 9.6 s at point (50, 48, 0).
    1.0000
Collision prediction correct: True
Colliding pair correct: [1, 2]
Collision time correct: 9.600s
Collision point correct: [50, 48, 0]

AI Reasoning: Centers lie on x-axis: x1(t)=0+20t, x2(t)=50+10t. Distance between centers = |x2-x1| = |50-10t|. Collision when distance = sum of radii = 3. Solve 50-10t=3 => t=(50-3)/10=47/10=4.7 s. At t=4.7 s: x1=20*4.7=94.0 m, x2=50+10*4.7=97.0 m. Contact point lies along the line between centers at distance 1.5 m from either center: x_contact = 94.0+1.5=95.5 m (y=z=0).

AI Reasoning: For each pair of spheres i,j, I formed the relative-motion vector p = p_i - p_j and relative velocity v = v_i - v_j. The squared center-to-center distance is D^2(t) = ||p + v t||^2, a quadratic in t. A collision occurs if D^2(t) <= (r_i + r_j)^2 for some t >= 0; the first contact time is the smallest nonnegative root of D^2(t) = (r_i + r_j)^2. I evaluated the quadratic coefficients and discriminants for all 6 pairs. All pairs except (1,2) have negative discriminants (no real roots) so they never reach the required separation. Pair (1,2) yields a positive discriminant and two real roots; the smaller positive root gives first contact at t ≈ 3.1030 s. At that time the centers are at C1 = (10t, 5t, 3t) ≈ (31.0300, 15.5150, 9.3090) and C2 = (50-5t, 25-2.5t, 15-1.5t) ≈ (34.4850, 17.2425, 10.3455). The contact point is along the line between centers at distance r1 = 2 m from C1 (and r2 = 2 m from C2), which for equal radii is the midpoint of the centers: (≈32.7575, 16.3787, 9.8272).
    1.0000

AI Reasoning: For each pair of spheres I formed the relative motion r(t)=r0+v t (r0 = p_j-p_i, v = v_j-v_i) and solved |r(t)|^2 = (ri+rj)^2. That gives a quadratic a t^2 + b t + c = 0 with a = v·v, b = 2(r0·v), c = r0·r0 - (ri+rj)^2. A real root t >= 0 indicates the pair touches/overlaps at that time. I evaluated the quadratic discriminant (b^2 - 4 a c) for every pair (28 pairs). Every pair produced a negative discriminant (or otherwise no nonnegative root), so no pair reaches the contact distance at any t >= 0. Therefore no collisions occur.

AI Reasoning: I treated each pair of spheres i,j by writing the relative motion r(t) = p_i - p_j + (v_i - v_j) t and solving |r(t)|^2 = (r_i + r_j)^2. That leads to a quadratic a t^2 + 2 b t + c = 0 with a = |v_i - v_j|^2, b = (p_i - p_j)·(v_i - v_j), c = |p_i - p_j|^2 - (r_i + r_j)^2. A collision occurs iff this quadratic has a real root t >= 0 (or c <= 0 at t=0 for initial overlap). I evaluated these conditions for every pair (all 66 pairs). No pair produced a real nonnegative root and no pair was initially overlapping. The closest approach I found among all pairs was for objects 9 and 10: their time of closest approach is t ≈ 6.14 s and the squared separation at that moment is ≈136 m^2 (separation ≈ 11.66 m), while their sum of radii is 5.3 m (squared = 28.09 m^2). Since 11.66 m > 5.3 m, they do not collide. Every other pair has an equal or larger minimum separation. Therefore no collisions occur for t >= 0.

AI Reasoning: Spheres collide when the distance between centers equals the sum of radii. For each pair i,j with relative position dp = p_i - p_j and relative velocity dv = v_i - v_j we solve (dv·dv) t^2 + 2(dp·dv) t + (dp·dp - (r_i+r_j)^2)=0 for the smallest nonnegative root. Evaluating all 28 pairs shows the earliest contact is between Object 1 and Object 2. They move directly toward each other along the x-axis: initial center separation 100 m, combined radii 4 m, closing speed 20 m/s, so time to contact t = (100 - 4)/20 = 96/20 = 4.8 s. Their centers at that time are at x=48 m and x=52 m, so the surfaces touch at x=50 m (y=z=0). All other pairwise collision times are later (the next earliest collisions occur around ~9.29 s and ~9.48–9.73 s).

    Test 40: Mirror Reflection.

Typical Prompt:

You are in a room with mirrors on some walls. Objects are placed at various positions.
You are standing at a specific location, looking in a specific direction.


Room: 10m x 10m x 3m (X: 0-10, Y: 0-10, Z: 0-3)

Mirror: On the wall at X=0, covering Y: 2-8, Z: 0.5-2.5
  - Normal pointing in +X direction
  - Named "Wall Mirror"

Objects:
- Red Ball at (3, 5, 1)
- Blue Cube at (7, 3, 0.5)
- Green Cone at (5, 8, 1.5)

Your position: (8, 5, 1.5)
Looking direction: toward the mirror (-X direction)


For each mirror visible from your position:
1. What objects can you see reflected in that mirror?
2. Where does each object appear to be located (as seen in the mirror)?


    Prompt Changes:
Various mirror configurations		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: Simple wall mirror
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Tests understanding of mirror reflections in 3D space.



Key concepts:

    

  • Reflection creates a virtual image at equal distance behind the mirror
    • 

  • Line of sight must intersect the mirror surface
    • 

  • Mirror bounds limit what can be seen
    • 

  • Multiple mirrors create multiple viewpoints
    • 


This spatial reasoning skill is essential for understanding optics, 
room layout, and many puzzle types.


AI Reasoning: I used the planar-mirror (virtual-image) method: reflect each object across the mirror plane (X=0) to get its virtual image. If the line from your eye to that virtual image intersects the mirror rectangle (Y in [2,8], Z in [0.5,2.5]) then that object is visible in the mirror. I computed the intersection points on the plane X=0 for each object’s virtual image; all three intersection points lie inside the mirror bounds, so all three objects are visible. I also checked your own virtual image (reflection across X=0) and found the reflection point on the mirror lies inside the mirror area, so you can see yourself.
    1.0000

To check visibility I computed the line from your eye to each virtual image and found where that ray hits the mirror plane; if that intersection lies inside the mirror bounds the reflected object is visible. I also checked your reflected eye position the same way to determine whether you can see your own reflection.

Parameters: Floor mirror

AI Reasoning: I treat the floor mirror as a flat mirror in the plane z=0. For a point object at (x,y,z) the virtual image seen in that mirror is at (x,y,-z). An object is visible in the mirror from the eye position E if the straight line from E to the image I intersects the mirror plane z=0 at a point inside the mirror footprint (X in [3,7], Y in [3,7]). I computed the intersection point M for each object by finding t so that E + t*(I - E) has z=0 (t = 1.6/(z_object + 1.6)), then checked M_x and M_y against the mirror bounds. I assumed objects are point-like (no blocking/occlusion by object volume). Results below include the image positions and the intersection points that show they land on the mirror surface.

Parameters: Angled mirror
View exact prompt

AI Reasoning: Mirror plane: x + y = 5 (normal (1/√2,1/√2,0), through (5,0,0)). Reflection across this plane maps any point (x,y,z) → (5-y, 5-x, z).

Reflected (apparent) positions:
- Vase (2,2,1) → image at (3, 3, 1).
- Clock (8,1,2) → image at (4, -3, 2).

Visibility test (image-method): a reflected object is visible if the line from the observer to the object's image intersects the mirror plane at a point within the mirror bounds (Y in [0,5], Z in [0,3]).

Vase: line from observer (3,6,1.5) to image (3,3,1) intersects plane at P_v = (3,2,0.8333...). This has Y=2 (within 0–5) and Z≈0.833 (within 0–3) ⇒ Vase reflection is visible.

Clock: line from observer to image (4,-3,2) intersects plane at P_c = (3.5,1.5,1.75). Y=1.5 and Z=1.75 are within bounds ⇒ Clock reflection is visible.

Self-reflection: reflecting the observer gives (-1,2,1.5); the observer→image line meets the plane at (1,4,1.5), which lies within mirror bounds, so you can see your own reflection.

AI Reasoning: For each planar mirror I computed the virtual image of each object by reflecting the object's coordinate across the mirror plane (mirror-image formula). Then I checked that the straight line from your eye to that virtual image intersects the mirror plane at a point inside the rectangular mirror bounds (if it does, the reflected sight-line hits the mirror and you can see the object's image there). All eight objects produce intersections inside each mirror's bounds, so every object is visible in every one of the three mirrors. Below I list, for each mirror, which objects are visible and the apparent (virtual image) location of each object as seen in that mirror (i.e. the object's mirror-image coordinates).

    Test 41: Laser beam art
    Score: 0.23 / 4
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

You are an artist in charge of creating a laser beam art piece.

You have a workspace of 100x100 meters, and a laser emitter at the center (50,50), facing -y.

You have an unlimited budget for mirrors and beamsplitters. These are both 20cm wide and neglicably
thin. Mirrors are essentially perfect, reflecting 100% of the beam energy. Beamsplitters are
essentially perfect, splitting the beam energy 50/50, 50% continues on it's existing path,
50% is reflected as if it was a mirror.

You should aim to fill your entire workspace with the art if possible, and you're free to choose the
optimal viewing angle for the audience to see your work.

The artwork you have been asked to create is:


A regular pentagram, with a line connecting each vertex to the center dividing the space into 5 identical triangles.

    Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought


AI Reasoning: Design summary
- Use the single central laser (at (50,50), pointing down, i.e. direction (0,-1)).
- Create five radial beams that go from the center to the five vertices of a regular pentagon of radius 45 m (centered on the laser). One radial beam is the transmitted central beam (straight down). Four additional radial beams are produced by placing a small cascade of beamsplitters directly in the initial beam path; each beamsplitter transmits part of the beam forward and reflects part into one of the other four vertex directions.
- At each pentagon vertex place a beamsplitter that picks off the incoming radial beam and reflects 50% into the chord that forms the pentagram edge (each vertex reflects into the vertex that is two steps ahead around the pentagon, producing the star cycle). The chord beam travels to the next vertex beamsplitter and is partially reflected on to the next chord, closing the pentagram.
- The radial beams (center→vertex) together with the chord (vertex→vertex) reflections produce the requested figure: a regular pentagram plus the five spokes from each vertex to the center (the five identical triangles). Choosing pentagon radius R = 45 m fills nearly the entire 100×100 workspace while leaving a comfortable margin.
Why this works
- A beamsplitter reflects exactly like a mirror for its reflected output, so placing one at each vertex redirects the incoming radial beam onto the correct chord. The reflected chord then reaches the next vertex where the vertex beamsplitter continues the pentagram cycle.
- Putting several beamsplitters in series along the initial downward beam lets us split the central beam into the four additional radial beams; the transmitted portion that passes all central beamsplitters becomes the downward radial beam to the bottom vertex.
- All components are 0.2 m wide and negligibly thin; positions are specified so the beam path hits each beamsplitter or vertex beamsplitter cleanly.
Notes
- I used exact regular-pentagon geometry (5-fold symmetry) with one pentagon vertex directly in the initial -y direction so the emitter’s transmitted beam naturally serves the bottom radial spoke. All angles and normals below are the unit directions required for the intended reflections.
- Mirrors are not required beyond the beamsplitters used here; the design uses beamsplitters exclusively (they both transmit and reflect).
    0.2283

Reference Volume:32.72
    
Intersection Volume:32.72
    
Difference Volume:0.00

    Test 42: Can you navigate a maze from photos only?
    Score: 0.00 / 3
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

You have to navigate a maze from the red sphere to the blue sphere. You are not given the maze, but you
are given the following aerial photographs of the maze, which gives enough information to navigate the
maze. 

Return the path from the red sphere to the blue sphere using the following notation:

w - take a step west
n - take a step north
e - take a step east
s - take a step south

So "ennne" means: go east, then turn north, north again, north for a 3rd step, then turn east.

If you are unable to solve the full maze, a partial solution is appreachiated.

Be sure to not prefix your answer with anything other than the path, 
as any character other than e, n, s, or w will be graded as incorrect.

    Test 43: Reconstruct a 3D voxel grid from photos
    Score: 0.01 / 6
  

  

    
Typical Prompt:

You are given 8 photographs of a 3D voxel grid taken from the 8 corners of the viewing cube.
Your task is to reconstruct the exact positions and colors of all voxels in the grid.

The grid is 4x4x4, with coordinates from (0,0,0) to (3,3,3).
There are 10 voxels in total.

Available colors: red, green, blue

For each voxel, provide its xyz coordinates and color name.

Coordinate system:
- X increases to the right (labeled +X)
- Y increases going back (labeled +Y)  
- Z increases going up (labeled +Z)
- (0,0,0) is at the front-left-bottom corner
- the white sphere is at the front-left-bottom corner of the 0,0,0 voxel.



    Prompt Changes:
Increasing grid size and voxel count		
  

  

    Subpass 0
Parameters: 4x4x4 grid with 10 voxels (3 colors)
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Can you reconstruct a 3D voxel grid from photos only?



Given 8 photographs of a colored voxel grid taken from the corners of a viewing cube,
reconstruct the exact positions and colors of all voxels.



This tests spatial reasoning, 3D reconstruction from 2D images, and color recognition.


AI Reasoning: I examined the eight corner views to identify a compact cluster centered near the middle of the 4×4×4 grid with one voxel elevated above the main group. From the repeated relative positions of green (center-heavy), blue (to the sides), and red (a mix of base and one high voxel) across views I inferred a 3-level grouping: a mainly-occupied central 2×2 area at z=1 (with green in the center and blues on two sides), a small base footprint at z=0 (mostly red with a blue at one corner), and a single red voxel raised above the cluster at z=2. The assignment below matches the counts seen in the images (red 4, green 3, blue 3) and arranges the voxels to reproduce the relative overlaps and occlusions visible from the eight corners.
